coredata.dc:title: Orange fluorescent dual-mode nanoprobe for selective and sensitive detection of amaranth based on S,N-Doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Xiang, Yingying
Tu, Yujiao
Jiang, Lei
Yuan, Lin
Liu, Ze
Xie, Qiang
Xiong, Xuequan
Song, Fei
coredata.dc:description: As one of the most widely used azo synthetic dyes, long-term consumption of excessive amaranthcan cause a threat to health. It is very urgent to establish high selective and sensitive method for amaranth detection. Orange fluorescent carbon dots utilizing l-methionine and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials, namely S,N-CDs, were prepared via one-step hydrothermal method. Dialysis, thin layer chromatography and chromatographic column were employed for further purification. The purified dual-mode nanoprobe based on fluorescent and UV absorption was successfully established. For fluorescent, excellent linear ranges of 5–70 μM and 110–195 μM were obtained, and low detection limit was 345.22 nM. For UV absorption, excellent linear ranges of 0–155 μM with low detection limit 12.70 nM. It revealed that inner filter effect and static quenching dominated in amaranth quenching the fluorescent of S,N-CDs. Furthermore, the dual-mode nanoprobe could be efficiently applied to determine amaranth in fruit juice, carbonated drink, and strawberry jam samples. It provided a new insight on the purification of carbon dots and detection of amaranth by dual-mode nanoprobe.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amaranth
Dual-mode nanoprobe
Inner filter effect
Static quenching
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitivity of amaranth detection based on dual-mode nanosensor
Possible quenching mechanisms of amaranth detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Scientific Research Fund of Yunnan Education Department
Yunnan Health Training Project of High Level Talents
Science and Technology Planning Project of Yunnan Science and Technology Department Joint Project of Local Colleges and Universities

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of Yunnan Health Training Project of High Level Talents ( H-2018086 ), Science and Technology Planning Project of Yunnan Science and Technology Department Joint Project of Local Colleges and Universities ( 202001BA070001-218 ), Scientific Research Fund of Yunnan Education Department ( 2022Y749 and 2022Y748 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart of S,N-CDs preparation and purification.
(a) TEM (inset: HR-TEM) and (b) FTIR spectrum (c) XRD pattern of S,N-CDs.
(a) XPS full scan spectrum of the prepared crude S,N-CDs. High-resolution C1s spectra (b), N1s spectra (c), O1s spectra (d) and S2p spectra (e).
(a) UV–vis, excitation and emission spectra of S,N-CDs, (b) Emission of S,N-CDs at various excitation wavelengths.
Fluorescence quenching efficiency of (a) metal ions, vitamins and food additive E123, (b) l-amino acids and food additives to S,N-CDs.
Fluorescence spectra of S,N-CDs with various amaranth concentrations (0–195 μM) (a).
UV–vis absorption of amaranth, S,N-CDs, S,N-CDs + amaranth (a) and decay lifetime of S,N-CDs, S,N-CDs + different concentration amaranth (b). UV–vis absorption of amaranth and Ex, Em of S,N-CDs (c).
Comparison of detection performance of the Amaranth with other sensors.
Analytical results of amaranth detection by S,N-CDs and HPLC in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Orange fluorescent dual-mode nanoprobe for selective and sensitive detection of amaranth based on S,N-Doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Stomatology, Yan′an Hospital Affiliated to Kunming Medical University, Kunming, 650031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yan′an Hospital Affiliated to Kunming Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Kunming University, Kunming, 650214, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Minimally Invasive Intervention, The Third Affiliated Hospital of Kunming Medical University, Kunming, 650118, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Third Affiliated Hospital of Kunming Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . Department of Minimally Invasive Intervention, the Third Affiliated Hospital of Kunming Medical University, Kunming, 650118, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one of the most widely used azo synthetic dyes, long-term consumption of excessive amaranthcan cause a threat to health. It is very urgent to establish high selective and sensitive method for amaranth detection. Orange fluorescent carbon dots utilizing l-methionine and o-phenylenediamine as raw materials, namely S,N-CDs, were prepared via one-step hydrothermal method. Dialysis, thin layer chromatography and chromatographic column were employed for further purification. The purified dual-mode nanoprobe based on fluorescent and UV absorption was successfully established. For fluorescent, excellent linear ranges of 5–70 μM and 110–195 μM were obtained, and low detection limit was 345.22 nM. For UV absorption, excellent linear ranges of 0–155 μM with low detection limit 12.70 nM. It revealed that inner filter effect and static quenching dominated in amaranth quenching the fluorescent of S,N-CDs. Furthermore, the dual-mode nanoprobe could be efficiently applied to determine amaranth in fruit juice, carbonated drink, and strawberry jam samples. It provided a new insight on the purification of carbon dots and detection of amaranth by dual-mode nanoprobe.

Schematic of the synthetic and purification route for orange fluorescent S,N-CDs and the applications in dual-model detection of Amaranth.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Food dyes, belonging to food additives, is added during the manufacture of products such as cakes, cookies, cheese, soft drinks, candies, ice cream, medicine, lipstick and cosmetics to improve appearance and taster. Usually, food dyes fall into natural and synthetic. The natural dyes derived from the animal, plant and microbiology, such as carotenoids, carotenes, riboflavins, chlorophylls, betalains, which usually benefit to human health. However, due to stability, low cost, easy handling and control over the intensity of the colourant, synthetic dyes are preferred by food industries. The raw materials for synthetic dyes are mainly chemical products, and they are composed of azo dyes and non azo dyes. Some azo dyes, containing aromatic rings and N=N bonds, can be cleaved into aromatic amines, which can cause cancer [1,2].

As a water-soluble azo synthetic red dye, amaranth (C20H11N2Na3O10S3, E123), namely 3-hydroxy-4-[(4-sulfonatonaphthalen-1-yl)diazenyl]naphthalene-2,7-disulfonate, is universally used in food and soft drinks for enhancing appearance and taste to attract consumers [3]. Amaranth is consisted of azo (N=N) functional groups, and aromatic ring structures. In the metabolic process of amaranth, toxicity is produced though reductive transformation of the azo bond by forming carcinogenic aromatic amines. It is recommended that a satisfactory daily dietary allowance of amaranth is 0–0.5 mg/kg [4–6]. It was reported that long-term consumption of food containing excessive amaranth may be harmful to human health, such as dizziness, cytostaticity, anxiety, neurotoxicity, and even cancer [7–10]. Hence, it is very important to establish selectivie and sensitive method for amaranth detection to guarantee food quality, safety and human health.

At present, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), near-infrared (NIR), Raman spectroscopy, and UV–vis are usually used to detect and analyze food dyes [11–14]. However, UV–vis has poor selectivity because of the interference from other compounds [15–17]. NIR is extensive due to the sensitive detector. The Raman analysis with a low analytical sensitivity [18]. HPLC is expensive instrument, specialized operator and toxic material from organic solvents. Similar to UV–vis spectroscopy, whereas fluorescence is 1-3 orders more sensitive. Thus, fluorescence is often chosen as a tool to detect the content of food dyes with high selectivity, simple sample preparation and easy operation [19].

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted people's attention due to small size, high fluorescence intensity and simple samples preparation [20,21]. Fluorescence CDs have been widely used for detecting heavy metal ions, environmental pollution, and as vectors for drug delivery [22–26]. Herein, a dual-mode nanoprobe was employed to detect the food dyes amaranth. It showed bright orange fluorescence, low synthesis cost, high quantum yield, high selectivity, and was used for detecting amaranth in real samples.

All analytical reagents were provided by Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), such as o-phenylenediamine (oPD), tartrazine (E102), new cooccine (E124), rhodamine B (Rb), trypan blue (TB), riboflavin (E101), amaranth (E123), congo red (CR), dimethyl formamidel (DMF), methanol, propanol, ethanol, acetone, CaCl2, AgNO3, CoCl2, Bi, Ni(NO3)2, FeCl3, HgCl2, CuSO4, Pb(NO3)2, MgCl2, BaCl2, KCl, Vitamin B3, Vitamin C, Vitamin E, l-arginine, l-valine, l-histidine, l-lysine, l-cysteine, l-isoleucine, l-alanine, l-methionine, l-tyrosine, l-tryptophan, l-phenylalanine, l-proline, l-serine, l-glutamine, l-glycine, l-threonine, l-glutamate, l-leucine and l-aspartic acid.

oPD and l-methionine (L-Met) was chosen as raw materials for preparing fluorescence CDs, namely S,N-CDs, via simple hydrothermal method. Briefly, 2.4 g L-Met and 1.65 g oPD dissolved in 20 mL DMF were mixed, and then transferred to the polytetrafluoroethylene high temperature reactor (190 °C, 4 h). The product was cooled naturally, centrifuged (13,000 g, 15 min) and filtered (0.22 μm filter membrane). The product was dialyzed (molecular weight cut off 1 KDa) against ultrapure water (48 h). The crude S,N-CDs powders were obtained after freeze-drying treatment, and characterized with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD).

For further purification, the crude S,N-CDs were submitted to silica gel column chromatography and gradient eluted with CH2Cl2 and MeOH (20:1 to 10:1). The eluent with orange fluorescence were collected and concentrated under vacuum to obtain the purified S,N-CDs, which characterized with Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Tecnai G2 F30S, FEI, Netherlands).

The effects of different solvents (DMF, propanol, acetone, ethanol and methanol), different proportions of L-Met and oPD (nL-Met/noPD = 0.87, 1.09, 1.30, 1.45, 1.74 and 2.17), different reaction temperatures (170 °C, 180 °C, 190 °C, 200 °C, 210 °C and 220 °C), different reaction times (2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h, 10 h and 12 h), on S,N-CDs were investigated. They were determined by the fluorescence intensity and UV absorbance value based on the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of S,N-CDs.

To estimate the coexisting interference, the selectivity was performed. Typically, 40 μL S,N-CDs solution was added with different volume of metal ions, amino acids, vitamins and food additives at the concentration of 1 mM. The mixture was diluted to 4.0 mL with sterile water. Fluorescence intensity of S,N-CDs was measured on the fluorescence spectrophotometer after 4 min at the slit width of 10 nm and 20 nm.

Typically, 40 μL S,N-CDs solution was added with different volume of amaranth solution (1 mM). The fluorescence intensity was measured under the conditions of optimal reaction time and pH value. The detection range was obtained by linear fitting and the detection limit was calculated.

Quinine sulfate was dissolved in H2SO4 (0.1 M). The QY value was 54% at excited wavelength 360 nm, which was appointed as a reference [27]. The equation as follows was used to calculate the QY of S,N-CDs: Q
CDs
= Q
R
(I
CDs
/I
R
) (A
R
/A
CDs
) (η
CDs
2
/η
R
2
)

Where the subscript CDs means S,N-CDs, the subscript R means reference; Q
CDs means QY value of S,N-CDs, I refers to the integrated emission intensity, η refers to the solvent refractive index (η
R = 1.33), and A means the absorbance value at the excitation wavelength.

The calculation of fluorescence lifetime (1) and the fitting model (2) is follow ：
(1)
τ =
∑
A n
τ n 2
A n
τ n
(
n = 1,2,3......
)
(2) Y= B + A
1 *exp(-i/τ
1 ) + … + A
n *exp(-i/τ
n ) (n = 1,2,3 …...)

In order to verify the practicality of the prepared S,N-CDs, fruit juice, carbonated drink, and strawberry jam samples were chosen for the same experiment. F0 value of 20 μL S, N-CDS diluted to 4.0 mL with sterile water was obtained at Ex 425 nm and Em 565 nm. The relevant physical properties of fluorescence, such as molar absorption coefficient and fluorescence emission peak are affected by the solution and incubation system [28]. Herein, the conditions in vitro incubation systems is very important, and the different incubation systems of each test sample as follows: 10 mL fruit juice added into 50 mL water, mixed, and filtered by 0.22 μm membrane. Carbonated drink, containing a large amount of carbon dioxide, was sonicated at 30 min, then filtered by 0.22 μm membrane. 3 g strawberry jam, belonging to semi-solid sample, was added into 10 mL MeOH/H2O (V/V, 1:1). The use of methanol is more conducive to the dissociation of pigment from the complex matrix of semi-solid sample [29], then sonicated for 10 min, centrifuged at 8000 g for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered by 0.22 μm membrane. 20 μL S,N-CDs was added to the treated samples of fruit juice (0.709 μM, 400 μL), carbonated drink (11.50 μM, 400 μL), and strawberry jam (400 μL). The mixture was diluted to 4.0 mL with sterile water. Fluorescence intensity (F value) of S,N-CDs was measured on the fluorescence spectrophotometer. Meantime, spiking experiment was performed as follows: a quantity of the amaranth standard solution 1 mM (5 μL, 10 μL, 20 μL) was added to the treated samples (400 μL) with 20 μL S,N-CDs; the mixture was diluted to 4.0 mL with sterile water, and fluorescence intensity was measured, then the recoveries of amaranth were estimated.

With simple hydrothermal method, S,N-CDs were prepared. Prepared crude S,N-CDs solutions were submitted silica gel column chromatography, and then gradiently eluted with CH2Cl2 and MeOH (20:1 to 10:1). The purified orange fluorescence S,N-CDs were obtained (Fig. 1 ). When the selected solvent, the proportions of L-Met and oPD, reaction temperature and reaction times were DMF, 1.45, 190 °C and 4 h, respectively, the maximum QY of S,N-CDs were reached (Fig. S1).

As one of the most common precursors for preparing red emissive CDs, oPD has been extensively studied. Lu et al. prepared six different oPD-based red CDs [30]. In this work, the orange S,N-CDs was obtained with oPD as precursor.

TEM was used to study the morphological features (Fig. 2 a). S,N-CDs had uniform spherical particles with the average diameter about 2.42 nm and the lattice spacing about 0.21 nm. A strong absorption peak at 3234.05 cm−1 displayed the presence of N–H and O–H bonds, the peak at 1617.99 cm−1 denoted the –C=O bonds; the peak at 1417.43 cm−1 indicated C–H bonds. The peak at 1103.08 cm−1 revealed C–N bonds, and the peak at 740.53 cm−1 presented N–H bonds by FTIR spectroscopy (TENSOR 27, Bruker, Germany) (Fig. 2b) [31]. The peak at 22.29 by XRD indicting the form of amorphous carbon structure after graphitization during high temperature (Fig. 2c).

C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p were associated with peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.6 eV, 531.6 eV and 163.0 eV, respectively, by XPS (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. U.S.A.) (Fig. 3 a). For C1s spectrum, three peaks at 284.75 eV, 286.0 eV and 288.0 eV, corresponding to C=C, C–N/C=N and C=O, respectively (Fig. 3b). Two peaks at 398.9 eV and 400.0 eV, attributed to –C=NH and C–N of N1s spectrum [32], respectively (Fig. 3c). For O1s spectrum, two peaks at 531.65 eV and 533.40 eV, corresponding to C=O and C–O–C [32], respectively. For S2p spectrum, three peaks at 163.0 eV, 164.0 eV and 167.0 eV, corresponding to Ph-S-Ph, S=C=S and SO2, respectively (Fig. 3d). Due to the relatively small peak areas, C–O–C can be observed in the O1s spectrum and S=C=S observed from the S2p spectrum, however, they were absent in the C1s spectrum (Table S1). XPS diagram was consistent with FTIR spectrum. It indicated that abundant hydrophilic and amino functional groups presented on the surface of S,N-CDs, and then possessed good water solubility. Therefore, S,N-CDs showed the characteristics of fluorescent nanoprobe and certain application potential.

Under sunlight, colorless liquids can be seen, while the orange liquids under UV excitation, so S,N-CDs were successfully prepared (Fig. 4 a, inset). It showed a strong absorption peak around 214 nm, attributed to the π-π* transitions, and a absorption peak around 268 nm, attributed to the n-π* transitions (C=C bond) (Fig. 4a) [33]. The emission wavelengths of the S,N-CDs was 565 nm, while the optimal excitation wavelength 425 nm (Fig. 4b). The present of stokes shift prevented the possibility of overlapping excitation and emission peaks, minimize distractions, and be help to the application of S,N-CDs in analysis and detection. Fluorescence intensity enhanced when excitation wavelength shifted (380–430 nm), and then lowered (440–480 nm). At the same time, the red shift of emission peak was observed, revealing wavelength dependent (Fig. 4b). Thus, it is very important for fluorimetry to correctly choice of working wavelength.

S,N-CDs fluorescence stability was studied with different salt concentration, pH, antioxidant performance, and response time under natural light and UV. The maximum quenching efficiency was obtained at pH 9.0, and fluorescence intensity of S,N-CDs remained stable at alkaline condition (Fig. S2a). S,N-CDs only slightly fluctuated with NaCl concentration increasing (0–2.0 mM) (Fig. S2b). There was almost no variation of fluorescence intensity after adding H2O2 (0–100 mM). It showed that S,N-CDs had better antioxidant performance (Fig. S2c). In order to study the effect of response time under natural light and UV on the fluorescence stability of S,N-CDs. Fluorescence intensity were estimated within 60 min under natural light. There was no significant change of fluorescence quenching efficiency, indicated that reaction time had little influence on the fluorescence response under natural light, thus S,N-CDs has strong photostability (Fig. S2d). There was a slight change of fluorescence quenching efficiency within 60 min under UV, indicating relatively stability under UV (Fig. S2e).
The QY of the S,N-CDs was measured to be 10.90% based on standard quinine sulfate (QY = 54%).

To investigate the selectivity of S,N-CDs, fluorescence intensity of different possible interfering substances including metal ions, amino acids, vitamins and food additives were monitored. The orange fluorescence of S,N-CDs could be efficiently quenched by only amaranth (Fig. 5 a-Fig. 5b). So, there was high selectivity between S,N-CDs and amaranth, and then amaranth could be detected by S,N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of amaranth detection based on dual-mode nanosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence intensity of S,N-CDs changed within pH 2.2–12 after adding amaranth, and the maximum quenching efficiency was obtained at pH 7.0 (Fig. S3a), so it was chosen as reaction pH. Fluorescence intensity of S,N-CDs was stable in 4 min, so it was chosen as reaction time (Fig. S3b).

The relationships between the fluorescence quenching efficiency and UV absorbance rate of S,N-CDs and amaranth concentrations were illustrated. With amaranth concentrations increased, fluorescence quenching efficiency of S,N-CDs decreased (Fig. 6 a). The fluorescence quenching efficiency of S,N-CDs was linear with amaranth concentrations (5–70 μM, R2 = 0.9963; 110–195 μM, R2 = 0.9955), and the low detection limit (LOD) was 345.22 nM (Fig. 6b). With amaranth concentrations increased, the UV absorbance rate of S,N-CDs enhanced (Fig. 6c). The UV absorbance rate of S,N-CDs was linear with amaranth concentrations (0–155 μM, R2 = 0.9984), and LOD was 12.7 nM (Fig. 6d).

There are few reports on the detection of amaranth by CDs, whereas more study on the detection of amaranth by electrochemical method [34–41] (Table 1 ). In terms of detection ability of S, N-CDs towards amaranth in the field of CDs, compared with other reports, the line range was wider with moderate LOD based on fluorescent, whereas good line range with lower LOD based on UV. The orange fluorescent S, N-CDs showed excellent dual-mode detection for amaranth.

Linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F0–F)/F0) of S,N-CDs and amaranth concentrations (5–70 μM, 110–195 μM) (b). UV absorbance spectra of the S,N-CDs with various amaranth concentrations (0–155 μM) (c). Linear relationship between UV absorbance rate ((A−A0)/A0) of S,N-CDs and amaranth concentrations (0–155 μM) (d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible quenching mechanisms of amaranth detection
ce:section.ce:para: Static quenching (SQ), dynamic quenching (DQ), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) are well known quenching mechanisms of quencher to fluorescence CDs [43].

UV–vis absorption of S,N-CDs, amaranth and S,N-CDs with amaranth were measured in order to confirm the quenching mechanisms of amaranth towards S,N-CDs (Fig. 7 a). It can be seen that the absorption of the S,N-CDs had no variation with or without amaranth.
Fluorescence decay lifetime showed no enhance without (2.76 ns) or with amaranth (2.75 ns) (Fig. 7b). As the result, IFE may be dominate the quenching process [44].
The absorption spectrum of amaranth was overlapped with the excitation spectrum of S,N-CDs (Fig. 7c). Therefore, IFE worked [44].

To prove IFE, Parker equation, as follow, was used [44].
F
c o r
F
o b s d
=
2.3 d
A
e x
1 −
10
− d
A
e x
10
g
A
e m
2.3 s
A
e m
1 −
10
− s
A
e m

F
cor refers to the observed fluorescent intensity, F
obsd refers to the corrected fluorescent intensity, A ex means the absorbance value at the maximum excitation wavelength, A em meana the absorbance value at the maximum emission wavelength, d refers to the cuvette width, s is the excitation beam thickness; g is the distance between the edge of cuvette and excitation beam [44]. With the increase of amaranth concentrations from 0 to 25 μM, F
cor
/F
obsd value enhanced (1.167–1.993) (Fig. S4). It was in accord with the Parker equation. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of amaranth to S,N-CDs fluorescent belonged to IFE.

Furthermore, Stern-Volmer equation was used to further investigate the kinetics of SQ mechanism [44]:
F 0
F
= 1 +
k q
τ
[ Q ]

F
0 is the PL intensity of S,N-CDs, and F is the PL intensity of S,N-CDs after addition of amaranth; Kq refers to the quencher coefficient (M−1‧S−1); [Q] refers to the amaranth concentration (μM); τ
0 refers to the FL decay lifetime of the prepared S,N-CDs (ns), τ
0 = 2.76 ns. A rise of temperature (288 K, 298 K, 310 K) leaded to the decrease of slope (0.393, 0.194, 0.170) and Kq (1.424 × 1014 M−1‧S−1, 7.029 × 1013 M−1‧S−1, 6.159 × 1013 M−1‧S−1) (Fig. S5). Kq value obtained in this study was higher than 2 × 1010 M−1‧S−1 (maximum value for scatter characterized by determining the values of Kq and the thermodynamic parameters by varying the collision quenching constant), so the quenching mechanism of amaranth was SQ.
Thus, it revealed that IFE and SQ dominated in amaranth quenching the orange fluorescent of S,N-CDs.

The orange fluorescent nanoprobe S,N-CDs was used to detect amaranth in real fruit juice, carbonated drink, and strawberry jam samples. The recovery rate of amaranth were estimated to fall within 99.3–104.0% based on fluorescent, and 98.5–102.6% based on UV absorption (Table 2 ), presenting the sensitivity of S,N-CDs on amaranth in fruit juice, carbonated drink, and strawberry jam (n = 3). The recovery rate of amaranth with S,N-CDs based on fluorescent was similar to HPLC. However, HPLC needed complex solution configuration, and the target could be detected with the help of more substances. The preparation process of S,N-CDs was simple and faster than HPLC. Therefore, S,N-CDs was chosen to detect amaranth in real samples.

In sum, the highly orange fluorescent nanoprobe S,N-CDs were prepared and purified. The purified S,N-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence characters. In addition, amaranth could be detected by dual-mode nanoprobe with excellent linear ranges and LOD based on orange fluorescent and UV absorption of S,N-CDs. Furthermore, the dual-mode nanoprobe could be efficiently applied for determination of amaranth in fruit juice, carbonated drink, and strawberry jam samples. Therefore, due to simple synthetic method, low cost, selective and sensitive, it is a good candidate for detecting amaranth in various real samples.

Yingying Xiang: Data curation, designed the experiment, Writing – original draft. Yujiao Tu: Methodology, Data curation. Lei Jiang: Methodology, Data curation. Lin Yuan: Data curation. Ze Liu: Data curation. Qiang Xie: Data curation. Xuequan Xiong: Data curation. Fei Song: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
